title-group.article-title: Elicited Repetitive Daily Blindness Associated With Gain-of-Function SCN1A Variants and Responsiveness to Sodium Channel Blockers

contrib-group.aff: 2CNRS UMR 7275, Institut de pharmacologie moléculaire et cellulaire (IPMC), Valbonne, France;
4School of Health and Wellbeing, University of Glasgow, United Kingdom;
5Paediatric Neurosciences Research Group, Royal Hospital for Children Glasgow, United Kingdom;
6Clinical and Experimental Sciences, University of Southampton, United Kingdom;
7Developmental Neurosciences, UCL Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health, London, United Kingdom;
8Department of Paediatric Neurology, University Hospitals Southampton NHS Trust, United Kingdom; and
9West of Scotland Centre for Genomic Medicine, Queen Elizabeth University Hospital, Glasgow, United Kingdom.

author-notes.corresp: Correspondence Prof. Brunklaus andreas.brunklaus@glasgow.ac.uk or Dr. Mantegazza mantegazza@ipmc.cnrs.fr

fn.p: The Article Processing Charge was funded by the authors.
Submitted and externally peer-reviewed. The handling editor was Editor Peter B. Kang, MD, FAAN.
These authors contributed equally to this work as corresponding author and co-senior authors.

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc. on behalf of the American Academy of Neurology.

license.license-p: This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-Non Commercial-No Derivatives License 4.0 (CCBY-NC-ND), where it is permissible to download and share the work provided it is properly cited. The work cannot be changed in any way or used commercially without permission from the journal.

sec.title: Background and Objectives
sec.p: Elicited Repetitive Daily Blindness (ERDB) is a debilitating condition resulting in recurrent, daily episodes of reversible visual loss. ERDB has been described in individuals with Familial Hemiplegic Migraine 3 (FHM3), caused by pathogenic SCN1A variants. Two families with cosegregation of FHM3 and ERDB (families 1–2) have previously been reported associated with SCN1A variants c.4467G > C/p.(Q1489H) and c.4495T > C/p.(F1499L). However, F1499L was also identified in a separate FHM3 family without ERDB. Thus, it is uncertain whether ERDB is a clinical feature in FHM3 besides the 2 identified families. We present 4 new families with cosegregation of FHM3 and ERDB resulting from 3 novel SCN1A variants. The proband in each family was commenced on sodium channel blocker medication. We performed functional analysis of the previously reported Q1489H and F1499L variants, including the overall effect in GABAergic neurons.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Data were collated through clinical interview and review of medical records for the probands of 4 new families with FHM3 and ERDB. Genetic analysis was performed for probands and, where possible, their parents. Whole-cell patch-clamp experiments were performed to measure sodium currents. Measurements were taken from transfected temperature sensitive SV40 Large T-antigen (tsA-201) cells and cortical GABAergic neurons in primary culture expressing the human Nav1.1 voltage-gated sodium channel with either wild type (WT), Q1489H, or F1499L variants.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Four new pedigrees are described with typical FHM3 and ERDB (families 3–6). Blindness attacks commenced from infancy to 9 years of age. Episodes occurred 3–10 times per day, lasting up to 30 seconds. Genetic testing revealed novel heterozygous variants in the SCN1A gene for family 3 (c.4027G > T/p.[A1343S]), 4 (c.5350G > T/p.[V1784F]), 5 (c.5006C > T/p.[A1669F]) and 6 (c.5006C > T/p.[A1669F]). Electrophysiologic testing revealed an overall gain-of-function on Nav1.1-mediated currents for the previously reported variants Q1489H and F1499L when compared with WT, particularly when they were expressed in cortical GABAergic neurons. In silico tools predicted gain-of-function in the 3 novel variants. All 4 probands responded well to treatment with carbamazepine reporting fewer migraines and blindness episodes.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Our study demonstrates that ERDB is a clinical feature in FHM3 patients because of gain-of-function Nav1.1 variants. Patients in this report responded to sodium channel blocker medications.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Elicited Repetitive Daily Blindness (ERDB) is a distinctive phenomenon observed in a subset of individuals with Familial Hemiplegic Migraine (FHM). It is characterized by brief, recurrent episodes of visual loss in one or both eyes, with complete recovery within seconds.1,2 ERDB has been reported in 2 families with autosomal dominant FHM, in whom heterozygous pathogenic variants in the SCN1A gene (c.4467G > C, p.Q1489H and c.4495T > C, p.F1499L) were identified, consistent with FHM type 3 (FHM3).1,2 By contrast, FHM1 and FHM2 result from pathogenic variants in CACNA1A and ATP1A2 genes, respectively.3 Not all SCN1A variant carriers with FHM3 exhibited ERDB, and the p.F1499L variant has also been described in a family with FHM3 without ERDB.4 Thus, ERDB appears to represent a rare, variably expressed feature within FHM3, and its prevalence and underlying mechanism remain uncertain.

SCN1A is one of the most frequently implicated genes in monogenic epilepsies.5-7 Most epilepsy-associated SCN1A variants cause loss of function (LOF) of the NaV1.1 voltage-gated sodium channel. Because NaV1.1 is predominantly expressed in GABAergic interneurons, LOF leads to interneuron hypoexcitability, reduced inhibition, and network hyperexcitability.8-12 Thus far, 12 SCN1A missense variants have been identified in families with FHM3.13-15 Previous studies have shown that FHM3-associated variants produce an overall gain of function (GOF) of NaV1.1, resulting in hyperexcitability of GABAergic interneurons.14,16,17 The magnitude of this GOF exceeds that seen with other NaV1.1 variants recently linked to severe, early-onset developmental and epileptic encephalopathies.18-20 Notably, we have shown that some FHM3 variants exhibit apparent LOF when mutant channels are expressed in cell lines, which converts to GOF when mutant channels are coexpressed with interacting proteins or studied in neurons.14,17 Recent studies from other laboratories have confirmed that FHM3-associated variants cause NaV1.1 GOF.21-24

Cortical spreading depolarization (CSD), a wave of hyperexcitability followed by long lasting depolarization of cortical networks, is the leading proposed mechanism of migraine pathophysiology.13,25 We suggested that Nav1.1 GOF could facilitate CSD initiation, as supported by experimental results and simulations obtained using a computational model.26,27 Therefore, genes implicated in FHM and their functional effects may help to elucidate the pathologic mechanisms of migraine and potential treatments.

The first study to investigate SCN1A variants associated with ERDB performed functional analyses of mutant NaV1.1 channels expressed in cell lines and Xenopus oocytes.23 Using computational modeling, it further predicted that GS967, an experimental sodium channel blocker, could normalize neuronal excitability in cells carrying these variants.23

Subsequent work in mouse models carrying the FHM3 variants L1649Q and L263V demonstrated responsiveness to GS967,24,28 and this compound partially rescued channel dysfunction caused by L263V expressed in cell lines.28 Although no targeted therapy exists for FHM3 or ERDB, these findings suggest that sodium channel blockers may represent a potential therapeutic approach.

We report 4 new pedigrees with FHM3 and ERDB resulting from 3 novel SCN1A variants not previously described. The index case from each pedigree was commenced on sodium channel blocker treatment and symptom control was monitored thereafter. In addition, we investigated the functional effects of the FHM-ERDB: familial hemiplegic migraine-elicited repetitive daily blindness (FHM-ERDB) variants Q1489H and F1499L, carried by the first 2 families reported in the literature to have ERDB. We expressed the variants in tsA-201 cell lines and cortical GABAergic neurons, as the neuronal cell background can modulate functional features of Nav1.1 variants14,17 and may better disclose specific features of FHM3-ERDB variants compared with pure FHM3 ones. The repeated study of variants enables us to compare their functional properties in different experimental conditions (cDNAs, cells) having as internal reference our previous functional studies of FHM variants.

sec.title: Patients
sec.p: Four novel pedigrees demonstrating cosegregation of FHM3 and ERDB are reported in this study (families 3–6). In this retrospective case report, patients presented consecutively through the routine clinical referral route. Clinical information was collected through structured interviews and review of medical records. Diagnosis of FHM was confirmed using the International Classification of Headache Disorders (ICHDs).29

sec.title: Genetic Analysis
sec.p: In each family, the proband underwent genetic analysis on genomic DNA obtained from blood samples. Parental testing was undertaken wherever possible to determine whether variants were inherited or occurred de novo. DNA was isolated from peripheral blood, and Sanger sequencing was performed under standard conditions using 28 primer pairs to amplify the 26 exons of the SCN1A gene (using the canonical transcript NM_001165963). The variant discovered in proband 5 was identified via a 104-gene panel designed to find genetic causes of paroxysmal events using targeted next-generation sequencing (confirmed with Sanger sequencing).30 Variants were classified using the UK Association for Clinical Genomic Science and the American College of Medical Genetics and Genomics (ACMG) guidelines.31,32 Further details are within the supplementary materials.

sec.title: Functional Analysis and In Silico Predictions
sec.p: The functional properties of the variants Q1489H and F1499L were evaluated using methods described in previous studies.14,16,17,33-35 In brief, we used the cDNA of the human NaV1.1 sodium channel expressed in the cell line tsA-201 or in mouse cortical neurons in primary culture. The canonical (unmutated) form of the NaV1.1 channel protein is indicated as “wild-type” (WT). Sodium currents were recorded with the whole-cell configuration of the patch-clamp 24–48 h after transfection. See supplementary material for more details.

In cases without biophysical experimental data, other tools were used to estimate function. Research has demonstrated that functional impact and variant locus within one Nav1.x channel gene can serve as a proxy for predicting effects in related sodium channels.19,36 If a missense variant identical to the one under investigation has been functionally characterized in a paralogous sodium channel, those findings are likely to be relevant. Thus, we used funNCion—an in silico prediction tool for functional variant prediction in Navs and Cavs ion channels to estimate biophysical properties.37 In addition, PER viewer was used to determine whether specific variant loci had previously been linked to GOF or LOF phenotypes.38

sec.title: Statistical Tests
sec.p: Data sets were tested for normal distribution with the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test and for homogeneity of variance with the Brown-Forsythe test. Outliers were detected and removed with the robust outlier removal method. Differences were tested with one-way analysis of variance (ordinary or Brown-Forsythe) followed by Dunnett T3 or Fisher least significant difference post hoc tests. Statistical tests were performed with Origin 10.0 or Prism 10.3. We considered differences statistically significant when p < 0.05. Significance is indicated in figures as: *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. If not otherwise indicated, data are shown as means ± SEM, “n” indicates the number of cells.

sec.title: Standard Protocol Approvals, Registrations, and Patient Consents
sec.p: The patients or their legal guardian gave their written informed consent for publication.
Authorization has been obtained for disclosure of any recognizable persons in information that may be published in the journal.

sec.title: Data Availability
sec.p: All data collected as part of this study are shown (anonymized) within the manuscript, tables, and supplementary materials.

sec.title: Clinical Description
sec.p: Findings for 6 families with FHM3 and ERDB are presented in Table 1 and Figure 1.

caption.p: Summary Table of Genetic Information, Clinical Characteristics and Response to Treatment of Proband 1 (Variant Q1489H) and 2 (Variant F1499L) Previously Reported in the Literature and Probands 3–6 Newly (Variants A1343S, V1784F and A1669V) Reported on in the Present Case Series

tr.td: Proband 1 (Le Fort et al. 2004 and Vahedi et al. 20091,2)
PM2, PP3, PP2, PM1, PP1_Str, PS4_Mod, PS3
PM2, PP3, PP2, PM1_Sup, PS4_Mod, PS3
PM2, PP3, PP2, PM1, PS4_Mod, PM5
Yes (focal epilepsy with focal motor onset)
11 mo (no further seizures after 2.5 y of age)
Scintillating scotoma, then unilateral paresthesia and weakness±aphasia. Followed by headache and nausea. Occur several times per mo
Unilateral/bilateral sensory and motor aura lasting 1–9hrs
Visual aura (minutes) then ipsilateral weakness and numbness lasting minutes- hoursOccur monthly
Unilateral weakness and numbness with apparent inability to talk, followed by headache. Episodes last 30–90 min
Unilateral weakness and numbness (including the face) followed by headache lasting for 1 hour
Unilateral numbness and weakness. Includes slurring speech. Last from minutes to hours. May be preceded by headache. More often headache afterwards for several hours
Eyeball pressure, orthostasis, change in light
Eyeball pressure, orthostasis, change in light
Orthostasis, eyeball pressure, change to light, patterns
Orthostasis, eyeball pressure, change to light, patterns, psychological stress
Orthostasis, eyeball pressure, change to light
Paroxysmal bursts of generalized spike and slow wave while drowsy and evidence of photosensitivity to flash at 2, 6, and 10Hz (20 months)Normal video telemetry (5y)

T2-WI hypersignal lesion that delimits the lateral territory of the right inferior cerebellar artery as well as a few small lesions compatible with ischemic sequelae in the left posterior inferior cerebellar artery territory
Normal (4 y)Nonspecific focus of signal abnormality in right cerebellar hemisphere and suggestion of generalized brain swelling. (6 y)
GS967—theoretical.23Le Fort et al.1 discuss successful migraine treatment with phenytoin, phenobarbital and primidone
No migraines reported. Reduction in ERDB >90%
Migraines now rare. Reduction in ERDB by 50% and episodes shorter
No reported migraines. Reduction in ERDB by 90%

fn.p: Abbreviations: ACMG = American College of Medical Genetics and Genomics; ERDB = Elicited Repetitive Daily Blindness; ERG = electroretinogram; GOF = gain of function.

fn.label: a
fn.p: The NM_001165963 transcript was used here in reference to Probands 3–6 (transcript information was not available for previously published Probands 1–2).

fn.label: b
fn.p: It is reported by both Le Fort et al.1 and by proband 4 in this study that seizures occurring are unrelated temporally with blindness or migraine attacks and are thought to be a separate phenomenon. Blindness attacks in ERDB are not thought to be epileptic. No previous reports or probands in our study have EEG reports with comments during any blindness episodes. It has been reported in proband 4 and 5 in this study, as well as by Le Fort et al.1 wherein early onset seizures remitted in individuals who would subsequently fit criteria for FHM.

caption.title: SCN1A Variants Associated With FHM3 and Pedigrees of the Families Carrying the Variants Reported in This Study
caption.p: (A) Schematic representation of NaV1.1 alpha subunit and localization of S/FHM-3 variants identified thus far; the variants Q1489H and F1499L that the authors have studied here are highlighted with arrows. Voltage-gated sodium channels are formed by 4 domains (DI-IV), each 1 formed by 6 transmembrane segments (S1-6) connected by intracellular and extracellular loops; within each domain, S1-4 form the voltage sensing module (in which the positively charged S4 is the voltage sensor) and S5-6 form the pore module, with the intracellular loop between S4 an S5 that is the linker between the 2 modules. The intracellular loop between DIII-IV is the inactivation gate, in which Q1489H and F1499L are located, blocks the pore in the fast inactivated states of the channel, acting as a hinged lid. (B) Six pedigrees with a cosegregation of FHM-3 and ERDB with families 1 and 2 identified in the literature and families 3–6 presented in the current manuscript. The pedigrees of family 3–6 were obtained via clinical interview with the proband and or parents. Probands are denoted by an arrow. Family members who suffer typical hemiplegic migraine are half shaded; those fully shaded suffer typical hemiplegic migraine and ERDB. ERDB = Elicited Repetitive Daily Blindness; FHM3 = Familial Hemiplegic Migraine 3.

sec.p: Family 1 is a three-generation Swiss family with FHM first reported in 2004.1 A c.4467G > C/p.(Q1489H) variant was discovered in the SCN1A gene for symptomatic family members, confirming FHM3.2 Of 6 family members with phenotypic FHM, 4 reported symptoms of a novel visual phenotype (Figure 1B). When interviewed, the proband (a 43-year-old man), described up to 10 attacks per day of complete visual loss “like a diaphragm closing and reopening.”1 Blindness attacks began in infancy and lasted up to 10 seconds. Attacks often resulted from trigger factors including orthostasis, changes in environmental light, and pressure to the eyeball(s). During adolescence, the proband began suffering from hemiplegic migraines, which were entirely independent of blindness attacks.1 MRI of the brain, electroretinogram (ERG), and visual evoked potentials were normal. Two family members (including the proband) had childhood epilepsy that resolved during adolescence.

Family 2 is a three-generation French family reported in 20092 (Figure 1B). Clinical assessment found 4 family members fitting criteria for FHM. Genetic testing of symptomatic individuals revealed the SCN1A variant c.4495T > C/p.(F1499L). The proband, an 18-year-old woman, was the singular family member to describe daily blindness episodes lasting up to 10 seconds. Visual loss occurred either unprovoked, or following triggers (orthostasis, eyeball pressure, and changes to environmental light). Episodes of hemiplegic migraine and ERDB began at 6 years, with no temporal relationship apparent between migraines and blindness attacks. MRI of the brain and ERG did not reveal any alternative cause for symptoms.

Family 3 is a two-generation UK family where 3 members are reported to have FHM (Figure 1B). The proband is a 21-year-old woman with a heterozygous c.4027G > T/p.(A1343S) SCN1A variant. Hemiplegic migraine commenced at 8 years. Migraines commenced with visual aura (typically zigzag lines in the right visual field). After which, there was ipsilateral sensory disturbance, dysphasia, and weakness in the ipsilateral limbs. The proband's father and paternal half-sister also experienced hemiplegic migraines, but they were not available for interview or genetic analysis. The proband reported daily blindness episodes from around 2 years of age, occurring up to 10 times daily and lasting 10–20 seconds each. Episodes occurred suddenly, involving either the entire visual field or sometimes partially obscuring it. Events were triggered by change in environmental light, seeing visual patterns, orthostasis, and eyeball pressure (such as rubbing the eyes). MRI of the brain, EEG, and ERG investigations were normal. EEG and ERG captured a blindness episode, and no abnormality was observed.

Family 4 is a three-generation UK family with 4 individuals reported to have hemiplegic migraine. Only the proband, an 8-year-old boy, had features of ERDB (Figure 1B). The proband and his father have a heterozygous SCN1A variant (c.5350G > T/p.[V1784F]) confirming FHM3. The proband described periods of bilateral visual loss as “black clouds coming in from the sides,” lasting 10–15 seconds. The phenomenon occurred 3–4 times per day, with full consciousness throughout. Triggers included eyeball pressure, changes in light levels, seeing patterns (moving stripes or zigzags for example), and orthostasis, with episodes described as temporally distinct from headaches/hemiplegia. Owing to orthostasis, he was unable to tolerate activities such as judo, trampolining, or rollercoasters. From 11 months to 2.5 years, the proband was treated for generalized tonic-clonic seizures, which have since ceased (EEG showed generalized spike and slow wave, Table 1). From 11 months, the proband also experienced hemiplegic migraines involving unilateral weakness and paresthesia of the upper and lower limb. This was often followed by a headache. EEG during episodes and MRI brain were normal. However, at 6 years, he experienced severe headaches associated with papilledema and pressure-related MRI changes (Table 1). Following a lumbar puncture demonstrating raised CSF opening pressure of 65cmH2O, he was successfully treated with acetazolamide. The father of Proband 4 also suffered typical hemiplegic migraine, with no history of blindness episodes.

Family 5 is a three-generation UK family with 5 individuals reported to have FHM. The proband, a 14-year-old girl, and her father report daily, bilateral loss of vision (Figure 1B). The proband and her father both have a heterozygous c.5006C > T/p.(A1669V) SCN1A variant. Proband 5 described rapid onset of bilateral visual loss occurring since age 9 years. There was often no trigger. Occasionally episodes related to changes in environmental light, orthostasis, or eyeball pressure. Visual loss lasted 20–30 seconds and occurred up to 10 times a day. There was a history of a single nocturnal seizure at 8 years. Proband 5 experienced only 2 episodes of hemiplegic migraine at ages 12 and 13 years. Blindness episodes were reported to be unrelated to headache. Extensive investigations were all normal (Table 1).

Family 6 is a three-generation UK family where 5 members are reported to have hemiplegic migraines, all reported to have symptoms of ERDB (Figure 1B). Proband 6 is an 18-year-old man with a heterozygous c.5006C > T/p.(A1669V) variant in the SCN1A gene. For as long as he can recall, he has experienced bilateral blindness occurring up to 3 times per day, lasting for approximately 3 seconds. Triggers for this include orthostasis, eyeball pressure, and shutting the eyes tightly. Typical hemiplegic migraines occurred approximately once every 3 months but were not related to a change in frequency of blindness episodes. There is no family history of epilepsy, and MRI of the brain was normal.

sec.title: Treatment Response
sec.p: For proband 1, initial reporting suggested reduced migraine frequency when treated with phenytoin, phenobarbitone, and primidone.1 No treatment was discussed for family 2.2 Each proband in families 3–6 was commenced on low dose carbamazepine and titrated up according to response. No side effects have been reported at the time of writing. Proband 3 was maintained on 16 mg/kg/d of carbamazepine at latest review. Blindness episodes had reduced from 10 times daily to 1–2 per week (90% reduction), with no migraines reported since treatment commenced. Proband 4 was maintained on 7 mg/kg/d of carbamazepine at latest review. He reported reduced ERDB episodes from 3-4 per day to 1–2 per day (50% reduction), with migraines rarely occurring. The severity of symptoms had previously required him to use a wheelchair due to periods of hemiplegia. Since commencing carbamazepine, the family reported that Proband 1 no longer required wheelchair support. Proband 5 was titrated up to 6 mg/kg/d carbamazepine and reported approximately 1 blindness episode per day compared with 10 times daily before treatment (90% reduction). She is a keen amateur footballer and can now participate without experiencing any blindness episodes. At latest review, proband 6 was taking 10 mg/kg/d carbamazepine and reported reduced blindness attacks by 90%, with no headaches since starting medication.

sec.title: Variant Classification and Prediction of Variant Function Using an In Silico Tool
sec.p: Using the ACMG classification, Q1489H, F1499L, and A1343S were classed as pathogenic, while V1784F and A1669V were classed as likely pathogenic (Table 1 for evidence criteria used). In silico tools predicted the variants A1343S, V1784F, and A1669V to have a GOF effect with probabilities of 66%, 61%, and 82%, respectively (Table 1).37 These novel variant positions are associated with GOF phenotypes when reported in paralogous sodium channels.38

sec.title: Mixed GOF and LOF Effects of the Variants Q1489H and F1499L in Cell Lines
sec.p: We investigated the functional effects of Q1489H and F1499L by patch-clamp whole-cell recordings of the human cell line tsA-201 transiently transfected with the WT or the mutant human Nav1.1 channel. Cells expressing the WT or mutant channels displayed a robust Na+ current (Figure 2A). However, Q1489H and F1499L induced a significant reduction in current density (Figure 2B; eTable 1; 28% for Q1489H, 27% for F1499L). As the overall functional impact depends on alterations of gating properties,14,16,17,21-23 these were studied next. Analysis of the conductance voltage plot (Figure 2C; eTable 1) showed just a mild modification in the slope of the activation curve for F1499L. The voltage dependence of fast inactivation showed a positive shift for both mutants (7.7 mV for Q1489H, 3.4 mV for F1499L; Figure 2C; eTable 1), consistent with a GOF.

caption.title: Effects of the Variants Q1489H and F1499L on Current Densities and Voltage Dependences in Transfected tsA-201 Cells
caption.p: (A) Representative whole-cell Na+ current traces recorded in cells expressing WT, Q1489H or F1499L hNaV1.1 channels. The currents were elicited in response to depolarizing steps from −60 to +120 mV in 10 mV increments from a holding potential of −120 mV. Scale bars 500 pA, 2 ms. (B) Mean maximum current density in tsA-201 cells transfected with WT, Q1489H or F1499L (C) Mean voltage dependence of activation and fast inactivation. Lines are mean Boltzmann fits. Data are shown as mean ± SEM. Mean values of the parameters and statistical comparisons are indicated in eTable 1.

sec.p: Comparison of mean current traces over a longer time scale (Figure 3A) showed an increase of the persistent current (INaP) for both mutants (Figure 3A). Quantification of the maximal INaP showed that Q1489H induced a 1.9-fold increase and F1488L a 1.8-fold increase, both consistent with a GOF, whereas quantification of INaP 20 minutes after the establishment of whole-cell configuration indicates that the increase is still present for Q1498H, but not F1499L (Figure 3B; eTable 1). This suggests that the increase in INaP for F1499L is likely induced by a modulation of the channel, whereas it is probably an intrinsic feature for Q1498H.

caption.title: Properties of Fast Inactivation and Persistent Current of WT, Q1489H, and F1499L in Transfected tsA-201 Cells
caption.p: (A) Comparison of the mean normalized WT, Q1489H and F1499L sodium currents elicited with a 100ms-long depolarizing pulse to +20 mV from a holding potential of −120 mV. The dotted line is the 0 level. Scale bar 5 ms. Left inset: display of the initial part of the currents. Scale bar 1 ms. Right inset: magnification to better show the persistent current (the transient current is truncated). Scale bar 10 ms. (B) Quantification of the persistent current (INaP) 5 minutes (left) or 20 minutes (right) after the establishment of the whole cell configuration. (C) Time constant of development of fast inactivation at the indicated potentials. (D) Time constant of recovery from fast inactivating using a 150-ms inactivating pulse at the indicated potentials. Data are shown as mean ± SEM. Parameters and statistical comparisons are displayed in eTable 1.

sec.p: We also studied the kinetics of development of fast inactivation (Figure 3C) and recovery from fast inactivation (Figure 3D) over a range of potentials. The curves of development of fast inactivation were well fit by a single-exponential relationship and showed that both Q1478K and F1499L induce an acceleration of the development of fast inactivation at all potentials tested (Figure 3C; eTable 1), which is a LOF consistent with neuronal hypoexcitability, because mutant currents can be inactivated by shorter depolarizations.

The curves of the kinetics of recovery from fast inactivation were well fit by single-exponential relationships (Figure 3D) and show that only the recovery of Q1489H was modified, displaying an acceleration at most potentials tested (e.g., 1.6-fold at −70 mV). This demonstrates a GOF, as the mutant can recover more rapidly after relatively short depolarization. During long depolarizations, sodium channels undergo slow inactivation, which is kinetically and mechanistically distinct from fast inactivation.5 During migraines, neurons undergo long-lasting depolarizations; therefore, properties of slow inactivation may play an important role in migraine pathophysiology. We investigated the properties of slow inactivation for both variants. The curves of the development of slow inactivation (Figure 4A; eTable 1), which were fit by a single-exponential relationship, showed that the mutants had a faster entry into the slow inactivated state than the WT (2.8-fold faster for Q1489H, 2.3-fold faster for F1499L), consistent with LOF. The voltage dependence of the development of slow inactivation did not show modifications for F1499L, but a positive shift and a shallower slope for Q1489H (Figure 4B; eTable 1), consistent with a GOF. The recovery from slow inactivation was well fit by the sum of 2 exponentials, being faster for both mutants, consistent with a GOF: the faster time constant of recovery was 1.6 and 3.9-fold faster for Q1489H and F1499L, respectively, whereas the slower time constant was 1.8 and 2.9-fold faster for Q1489H and F1499L, respectively (Figure 4C; eTable 1). The voltage dependence of the recovery was modified only by the mutant F1499L, which showed a positive shift of 9 mV (Figure 4D; eTable 1) consistent with a GOF.

caption.title: Properties of Slow Inactivation of WT, Q1489H, and F1499L
caption.p: (A) Mean development of slow inactivation at −10 mV, the line are mean fits of exponential relationships; (B) Voltage dependence of development of slow inactivation; the lines are mean fits to Boltzmann relationships. (C) Mean recovery from slow inactivation at −90 mV; the lines are mean fits of exponentials. (D) Voltage dependence of recovery from slow inactivation; the lines are mean fits to Boltzmann relationships. Data are shown as mean ± SEM. Mean parameters and statistical comparisons are displayed in eTable 1.

sec.p: Overall, our findings indicate that, while some functional effects are variant-specific, both Q1489H and F1499L exhibit mixed properties, displaying features of both GOF (consistent with neuronal hyperexcitability) and LOF (consistent with neuronal hypoexcitability).

sec.title: Overall GOF Effects of Q1489H and F1499L in Neocortical Neurons
sec.p: We have previously demonstrated that, for certain Nav1.1 variants, the neuronal cell background can mitigate the LOF effect caused by reduced current density, likely by rescuing trafficking defects, whereas gating properties remained comparable with those observed in tsA-201 cells.14,39 To investigate the functional effect of Q1489H and F1499L in a neuronal cell background, we transfected neocortical mouse neurons in primary cultures. To block endogenous sodium currents with the specific blocker tetrodotoxin (TTX) and selectively record the current of exogenous hNaV1.1 channels, we used hNaV1.1 clones that are TTX-resistant, as reported in previous studies.14,17,40 Thus, we recorded sodium currents with whole-cell patch-clamp recordings in the presence of 1 μM TTX from fluorescent (expressing yellow fluorescent protein as reporter) fusiform (GABAergic) neurons.14,17,41

Figure 5A shows representative sodium current families, showing that Q1498H and F1499L channels were rescued when expressed in neocortical neurons, as their current density was not reduced (Figure 5C left; eTable 2). This contrasts with the results obtained in tsA-201 cells, where reduced current densities were observed. We also investigated certain hNav1.1 gating properties in neurons to find out whether the modifications induced by Q1498H and F1499L were similar to those observed in tsA-201 cells. As shown in Figure 5, B and C right, 5D, and eTable 2, the persistent current was increased for both mutants (2.5-fold and 3.9-fold for Q1498H and F1499L, respectively), the voltage dependence of activation was not significantly modified and the voltage dependence of inactivation showed a significant positive shift (11 mV and 6.4 mV for Q1498H and F1499L, respectively). Therefore, like in tsA-201 cells, both Q1498H and F1499L destabilize the inactivated state of the hNav1.1 channel, inducing a GOF.

caption.title: Properties of Q1489H and F1499L Currents Elicited With Voltage Steps in Fusiform Bipolar (GABAergic) Neocortical Neurons
caption.p: (A) Representative whole-cell sodium current traces recorded with depolarizing voltage steps from −45 mV to +10 mV (5 mV increase) from a holding potential of −80 mV in the presence of 1 µM TTX from fusiform bipolar GABAergic neurons transfected with F383S-WT (top), F383S-Q1489H (middle) or F383S-1499L (bottom) hNav1.1 channels. Scale bars 200 pA, 1 ms. (B) Comparison of mean normalized current traces elicited with a command step to −10 mV from a holding potential of −80 mV (n = 9 for WT, n = 10 for F383S-Q1489H and n = 11 for F383S-1499L); the dotted line is the 0 level. (C) Current Density-Voltage (I-V) plot of the peak transient current (left), and mean current-voltage plot for INaP measured after 5 minutes from the establishment of the whole-cell configuration and expressed as percentage of the transient current (right). (D) Mean voltage dependence of activation and fast inactivation, lines are the mean Boltzmann fits. Data are shown as mean ± SEM. eTable 2 for mean values and statistical comparisons.

sec.p: Recordings from transfected neurons are more challenging than those from tsA-201 cells, often limiting comprehensive assessment of all gating properties. Nevertheless, especially in cases where mixed GOF and LOF effects are present, it is crucial to determine the overall functional impact of variants beyond individual gating alterations. We investigated the overall effect using action potential clamp experiments. Figure 6, B and C and D, shows action currents elicited expressing WT, Q1498H, or F1499L, respectively, and applying a fast-spiking neuron action potential discharge as voltage command (Figure 6A; eTable2). We compared the amplitudes of action currents expressed as current density, focusing on the first, second, and last action currents in the discharge. The amplitude of the first peak current was comparable with the WT for both mutants (Figure 6, E and F), whereas the peak amplitude of the second and final currents in the discharge showed a 2-fold and a 2.2-fold increase for Q1498H, a 1.7-fold and a 2-fold increase for F1499L, respectively. The action currents of the 2 mutants were not significantly different from one another. Thus, both Q1498H and F1499L exhibit a clear and quantitively similar overall GOF.

caption.title: Properties of Q1489H and F1499L Action Currents Elicited Applying Fast-Spiking Action Potential Discharges in Neocortical Fusiform Bipolar (GABAergic) Neurons
caption.p: (A) Whole-cell action sodium currents (displayed as mean current density) for WT (n = 9), Q1489H (n = 12) and F1499L (n = 8), elicited using as voltage stimulus an action potential discharge recorded in a neocortical mouse brain slice from a GABAergic fast-spiking neuron applied from a holding potential of −90 mV (top panel); scale bars 5 pA/pF, 100 ms. (B) Superimposed traces showing the first, second and last action currents in the discharges (displayed as mean current density) and plots showing the comparison of the amplitudes of the second and last action currents in the discharges (the first action current did not show statistical differences); scale bars 3 pA/pF, 1 ms. Data are shown as mean ± SEM. eTable 2 for mean values and statistical comparisons.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study reports clinical and genetic data for 4 new families with both FHM3 and ERDB, associated with 3 novel pathogenic SCN1A variants, alongside the functional study of 2 SCN1A variants previously identified in 2 families with cosegregation of these phenotypes.1,2 Our findings demonstrate that ERDB is a recurrent, homogeneous clinical phenotype associated with GOF SCN1A gene variants in FHM patients. Patients in this series responded well to sodium channel blocker treatment.

In the 4 new families reported, descriptions of both hemiplegic migraine and ERDB are highly stereotyped between cases. Affected family members available for testing in families 3–6 were found to have heterozygous SCN1A variants. In silico tools predicted all 3 novel variants identified here to result in a GOF.19,37 Probands in families 3–6 were commenced on carbamazepine, and all reported a marked reduction in symptoms. Both migraines and blindness episodes responded to treatment, suggesting that both phenomena result from GOF in the Nav1.1 channel.

All those affected by FHM3 and ERDB stated that blindness episodes were entirely distinct from migraines. We agree with the assertion of Vahedi and colleagues2 that descriptions of ERDB are significantly different from the ICHD definition of retinal migraine, which is typically unilateral, lasts >5 minutes, and is accompanied by a headache or other symptoms.29 Vahedi and colleagues suggest that isolated “retinal spreading depolarization” (RSD) may be a distinct pathophysiology explaining ERDB, given the expression of the Nav1.1 channel within the retina.2,42 Our data are consistent with this possible mechanism.

Two patients had a diagnosis of epilepsy: one with focal and one with generalized onset seizures. The observed phenotypes are different to the recently described SCN1A GOF developmental and epileptic encephalopathies disease spectrum.18,20

Pedigrees of families 3–6 suggest autosomal dominant inheritance of FHM, yet only certain affected individuals describe ERDB symptoms. In families 2, 3, and 4, the probands are the sole family members affected by ERDB and FHM3. In families 1, 5, and 6, several of those with FHM are also afflicted by blindness episodes (4/6, 2/4, and 5/5 family members, respectively). A family case report with FHM3 associated with the F1499L variant (the exact variant in family 2) has since been published, with no evidence of ERDB in those interviewed.4 Together, these findings suggest a variable phenotype, variable expressivity, or the impact of other unknown factors on ERDB occurrence. Findings gleaned from clinical interview with probands included their parents where possible. However, without the inclusion of older generations in interviews, there is potential for ascertainment bias, which may explain the appearance of anticipation of the ERDB phenotype in our pedigrees (Figure 1).

We performed functional investigations in both standard cell lines (tsA-201) and GABAergic neurons in primary culture for the 2 SCN1A variants associated with cosegregation of FHM and ERDB in families 1 and 2 (Q1489H and F1499L). We compared findings with previously published functional data for these variants obtained in different conditions, and with our own data obtained with other Nav1.1 variants. The results demonstrate that both Q1489H and F1499L cause mixed effects on Nav1.1 functional properties in the tsA-201 cell line: GOF (rightward shift of the voltage dependence of inactivation, increase of persistent current and faster recovery of slow inactivation for both mutants; faster recovery from fast inactivation and positive shift of the voltage dependence of development of slow inactivation for Q1489H; positive shift of the voltage dependence of recovery of slow inactivation for F1499L) and LOF (reduction of current density, faster development of fast inactivation and faster development of slow inactivation for both mutants). A previous study investigating functional properties of Q1489H and F1499L in cell lines also found mixed GOF and LOF effects.23 However, modifications in the functional properties were not identical because reduction of current density, positive shift of the voltage dependence of development of slow inactivation, acceleration of the development of fast inactivation, and acceleration of development of slow inactivation were not observed. These differences may be explained by the different NaV1.1 cDNA or by the different cell line used.23

It is important to assess the functional effects of variants within a neuronal cell background. Notably, in previous studies, we demonstrated that the reduction in current density of Nav1.1 variants, particularly FHM-associated variants, can be rescued by the neuronal cell background,14,17 probably because of improved trafficking. Thus, we expressed Q1489H and F1499L in GABAergic neurons in primary culture and observed that the reduction in current density was completely rescued for both mutants, whereas modifications in the gating properties investigated were similar to those observed in tsA-201 cells, as with variants previously studied.14,17 An important property used to evaluate the overall effect of variants is the current elicited by a neuronal discharge of fast-spiking GABAergic neurons as voltage stimulus. These action currents were increased for both Q1489H and F1499L by approximately 130%. Notably, this GOF was larger than observed in the pure FHM mutant L1670W (13.5% increase), previously studied under the same experimental conditions.14 Although this comparison is limited to few variants, it might be hypothesized that FHM-ERDB-associated NaV1.1 variants could show a larger GOF than pure FHM NaV1.1 variants in neuronal cells. Overall, tsA-201/human embryonic kidney cell lines and neurons should be employed in parallel for functional studies because tsA-201 cell lines facilitate detailed analysis of gating properties that are difficult to assess in neurons, while neuronal cultures enable more reliable quantification of current density, which may vary in a cell type-dependent manner.43 iPSC-derived neurons are not adapted for studying detailed gating properties and demonstrate variability in neuronal maturation.12

The functional properties of Q1489H and F1499L show that a major effect of the variants is inhibition of inactivation. Both variants are located in the D3-4 linker region of the SCN1A protein, an area important for inactivation of Nav1.x channels.44 The novel variants we identified (A1343S, A1669V and V1784F) are located in the IIIS4-S5 intracellular loop, the IVS4-S5 intracellular loop and the IVS6 segment, respectively. Notably, these regions have also been implicated in the process of inactivation.44 Overall, the effects of the variants are qualitatively in accordance with the FHM3 pathologic mechanism we previously proposed, involving hyperexcitability of GABAergic interneurons leading to increased extracellular K+, which could initiate CSD.26,27 Since Nav1.1 is expressed in the retina,42 SCN1A variants implicated in ERDB, in addition to modifications of neuronal brain excitability leading to CSD, may also alter retinal excitability and produce RSD,45,46 which may be the pathologic mechanism underlying ERDB.2 However, the very brief duration of ERDB episodes (typically <20 seconds) contrasts with the much slower kinetics of classical RSD, which usually evolves and recovers over several minutes. This temporal discrepancy suggests that, if RSD contributes to ERDB, it is likely to represent an atypical phenomenon (e.g., faster than classical RSD due to NaV1.1 GOF) or not a full-blown spreading event (such as a transient depolarization block of retinal ganglion cells).

Barbieri et al.23 modelled the effect of a sodium channel blocker on FHM3 and FHM-ERDB-associated NaV1.1 variants. Their model suggested that, theoretically, sodium channel blockers may reduce the hyperexcitability induced by these variants. More recently, we showed that sodium channel blockers GS967 and carbamazepine partially rescue the dysfunctions caused by the L293V FHM3-associated variant on the functional properties of Nav1.1 in cell lines28 and demonstrated their effectiveness in vivo on homozygous mouse models carrying this variant.28 Moreover, it has been shown that GS967 reduces mortality in homozygous mice carrying the FHM3-associated variant L1649Q.24 This provides a scientific basis for treatment of FHM3 and ERDB with sodium channel blockers.

Wider access to genetic testing and improved functional understanding of pathogenic variants can support therapeutic decision making. This has already been successfully applied to determining treatment in voltage-gated sodium channelopathies.19 While predictive tools can aid the categorization of new sodium channel neuronal gene variants,37 the complex interplay of various factors means that predictions do not always match biophysical properties of sodium channels or indeed the clinical phenotype.19 Therefore, patch-clamp studies remain an important method for elucidating the functional impact of pathogenic variants.

The functional characterization of the other 3 identified variants was not performed in this study, which represents a limitation. Our primary objective was to focus on variants previously characterized, enabling direct comparison across different cellular contexts, including cortical GABAergic neurons, and with experimental conditions used in prior work. Future studies will extend this analysis to the remaining variants to fully elucidate their biophysical properties and contribution to the FHM3-ERDB spectrum.

In conclusion, we have characterized the functional effect of 2 SCN1A genetic variants linked to the FHM3-ERDB phenotype, demonstrating that both confer a net GOF on Nav1.1-mediated currents in cell lines and cortical GABAergic neurons. We further report the clinical profile of 4 new families containing individuals with an FHM3-ERDB phenotype, each harboring 1 of 3 distinct SCN1A gene variants; in silico prediction tools confirm GOF effects, consistent with the observed clinical manifestations. Each proband reported a marked reduction in symptoms when treated with carbamazepine, in keeping with a GOF phenotype. Overall, our study demonstrates that ERDB is a clinical feature of FHM3 patients caused by GOF Nav1.1 variants, responsive to sodium channel blockers.

ack.title: Acknowledgment
ack.p: The investigators would like to thank all of the patients and families involved.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: S. Cestèle: drafting/revision of the manuscript for content, including medical writing for content; major role in the acquisition of data; study concept or design; analysis or interpretation of data. sec.title: Study Funding
sec.p: This work has been supported by funding from the French government, through the France 2030 investment plan managed by the Agence Nationale de la Recherche (ANR), as part of the Université Côte d'Azur's Initiative of Excellence (IdEx) Jedi (ANR-15-IDEX-01) and by the Laboratory of Excellence “Ion Channel Science and Therapeutics” (LabEx ICST ANR-11-LABX-0015-01, France) to M Mantegazza.

sec.title: Disclosure
sec.p: M Mantegazza's laboratory is a member of the “Fédération Hospitalo-Universitaire” InovPain (FHU-InovPain, France); A Harper receives PhD funding from the SCN1A Horizons study. Full disclosure form information provided by the authors is available with the full text of this article at Neurology.org/NG.

def.p: American College of Medical Genetics and Genomics
